title: Quiz: Error-Correcting Codes Fundamentals
description: Test your understanding of linear codes and their cryptographic applications

questions.question: For a linear [n, k, d] code, what is the error correction capability t?
questions.explanation: A code with minimum distance d can correct up to t = ⌊(d-1)/2⌋ errors. This is because if two codewords differ in at least d positions, any pattern of t errors leaves the corrupted word closer to the original than to any other codeword.

questions.question: What is the relationship between the generator matrix G and parity-check matrix H?
questions.explanation: The generator matrix G (k×n) and parity-check matrix H ((n-k)×n) satisfy G·H^T = 0. This ensures that every codeword c = m·G satisfies H·c^T = 0.

questions.question: Why is the syndrome s = H·r^T useful for decoding?

options.text: It directly gives the transmitted codeword
It depends only on the error pattern, not the codeword
It's always zero for received words

questions.explanation: For r = c + e, we have s = H·r^T = H·c^T + H·e^T = 0 + H·e^T = H·e^T. The syndrome depends only on the error pattern e, so we can use it to identify and correct errors without knowing the original codeword.

questions.question: What makes decoding random linear codes computationally hard?

options.text: The matrices are too large to store
The syndrome decoding problem is NP-complete
There are too many possible messages

questions.explanation: The syndrome decoding problem (finding a low-weight error vector matching a given syndrome) is NP-complete for random linear codes. This was proven by Berlekamp, McEliece, and van Tilborg in 1978.

questions.question: In Information Set Decoding (ISD), what is the basic strategy?

options.text: Brute force all possible error patterns
Guess error-free positions and solve a linear system

questions.explanation: ISD guesses k positions that are error-free (the information set). If correct, the remaining n-k positions contain all errors, and a linear system can identify them. The probability of correct guess determines the complexity.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of Goppa codes for cryptography?

options.text: Efficient secret decoding while appearing random publicly

questions.explanation: Goppa codes have algebraic structure enabling efficient decoding (Patterson's algorithm), but when disguised by scrambling and permutation, they appear random. This asymmetry is the security foundation of McEliece.

questions.question: For a [7, 4, 3] Hamming code, how many errors can be corrected?
questions.explanation: With minimum distance d=3, the error correction capability is t = ⌊(3-1)/2⌋ = 1. The Hamming [7,4,3] code can correct any single bit error.

questions.question: What is the main drawback of using Goppa codes in the McEliece cryptosystem?
questions.explanation: McEliece with Goppa codes requires storing a large random-looking matrix as the public key, typically hundreds of kilobytes to megabytes. This is the main practical limitation.

questions.question: Quasi-cyclic codes (used in BIKE) have much smaller public keys than Goppa codes because their structure can be compactly represented.
questions.explanation: True! Quasi-cyclic codes have circulant structure where each row is a cyclic shift of the previous. This means the entire matrix can be represented by just one row, reducing key sizes from megabytes to a few kilobytes.
